html:
Vmoto Limited (ASX: VMT)

Vmoto is the kind of micro-cap that looks statistically ugly just before it becomes interesting. At A$0.120, the equity is worth about A$48 million. Using the company’s 31 March 2026 cash of A$31.7 million and A$12.9 million drawn bank facility, the operating business is being valued around A$29 million to A$32 million, or roughly 0.35x to 0.43x management’s FY26 revenue guidance of A$73.5 million to A$85 million.

The backward-looking screen is terrible: FY25 revenue fell to A$46.8 million, EBITDA loss was A$5.7 million, net loss was A$10.0 million, and operating cash outflow was A$5.0 million in the FY25 annual report. That is why the stock is still treated like a failed EV scooter story. But the current setup is not about FY25. It is about whether FY25 was the trough after Europe subsidy weakness, high rates, China discounting, and internal restructuring.

The inflection evidence is now real. Vmoto guided FY26 sales revenue to A$73.5 million to A$85 million, up 57% to 82% from FY25, citing increased orders from existing and new customers in its 9 April 2026 guidance. Then 1Q26 delivered 6,693 units, up 127% year over year and 46% sequentially, with positive operating cash flow and a 7,133-unit firm order book scheduled for 2Q26 and 3Q26 in the 1Q26 update. At FY25’s average revenue per vehicle of about A$3,700, 1Q deliveries plus the end-quarter order book represent roughly A$51 million of potential revenue before new orders later in FY26. That makes guidance look ambitious but not promotional.

The market is missing that Vmoto is no longer simply a low-margin export scooter assembler. It is trying to become a fleet electrification platform for two-wheel delivery mobility: vehicles, battery swapping, fast charging, financing, IoT, fleet software, and recurring service models. The company has deliberately exited the hyper-competitive China domestic sales market, restructured European subsidiaries, built Thailand assembly capability, and targeted B2B fleets rather than chasing consumer volume. Named relationships include Uber Europe, Zenion, Domino’s, Burger King, Malaysia Post, Thailand partners, Vietnam exposure through Lit Energy, and a small Pakistan JV. None of these is individually enough to underwrite the stock, but together they show the transition from discretionary scooter sales to fleet infrastructure.

The MotoGP agreement is not the core thesis, but it matters because brand trust is one of the missing ingredients in electric two-wheelers. Vmoto will produce three MotoGP edition electric scooters globally, gains MotoGP Electric Scooter Supplier status, gets product placement at European events, and can showcase charging and swapping infrastructure through the arrangement until 2030, per the 4 May 2026 announcement. The stock jumped on the news, then faded. That is useful: the market is not yet capitalizing the deal as if it guarantees anything. If actual MotoGP-branded sales appear in orders, the rerating has not been spent.

The operating leverage is the point. FY25 gross margin was still about 29% despite a weak year. If FY26 revenue lands near the midpoint of guidance, revenue rises by roughly A$32 million. At a similar gross margin, that adds about A$9 million of gross profit against a cost base already carrying Thailand setup, European restructuring, Nanjing facility spend, and FY25 credit and inventory charges. A business that lost A$5.7 million at EBITDA in FY25 can plausibly swing to A$3 million to A$7 million EBITDA in FY26 without heroic margin assumptions. If revenue later returns toward A$100 million, Vmoto does not need SaaS margins. It only needs mid-single-digit to high-single-digit EBITDA margins, which it already achieved in the stronger FY21 to FY23 cycle.

Downside protection is imperfect but real. Cash is still high, net cash is around A$16 million to A$19 million depending on lease treatment, and FY25 net tangible assets were about A$60 million, above the current market cap. The company also repurchased 39.5 million shares at A$0.12 in 2025. The governance scar is obvious: management attempted to delist from ASX, shareholders resisted, and the Takeovers Panel history shows why minority investors became skeptical of the register and board process. That overhang is part of why the stock is cheap. It is also why the rerating can be sharp if the company remains listed and starts producing cash.

The bear case is that management has simply moved the story from scooters to “solutions” while the economics remain commodity hardware. A miss against FY26 guidance, weak gross margin, another receivables problem like the FY25 doubtful debt charge, or cash falling below A$25 million gross would damage the thesis. A discounted raise would break it. The other risk is that order timing flatters 1Q26 cash flow because customer deposits came in ahead of deliveries. The next proof point is not revenue alone. It is revenue conversion with gross margin near 28% to 30% and no renewed working capital blowout.

The variant perception is simple: the market is valuing Vmoto as a shrinking EV hardware micro-cap with governance baggage. The more accurate forward view is a cash-backed, under-followed fleet electrification platform with a global distribution footprint, improving order book, current-year revenue growth guidance above 50%, and enough operating leverage to become cash-flow positive without new equity. That is a very different security.

Using A$0.120 as the current stock price:

Bear target: A$0.06, down 50%. FY26 revenue misses at A$50 million to A$55 million, EBITDA remains negative, cash falls, and the market values the business near 0.3x depressed sales.

Base target: A$0.22, up 83%. FY26 revenue lands around A$78 million, EBITDA turns modestly positive, operating cash flow is near breakeven or better, and the market applies about 0.85x forward sales plus net cash.

Bull target: A$0.45, up 275%. FY26 guidance is achieved, FY27 revenue visibility moves above A$100 million through B2B fleets, MotoGP editions, and service revenue, EBITDA reaches A$10 million plus, and the market rerates Vmoto to about 1.2x to 1.4x forward sales.
